content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 30,
1992
Douglas
D.
Durnhart
Management Assistant
I
City of
San Juan Capistrano
32400 Paseo Adelanto
San Juan Capistrano,
CA
92675
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—524
Dear Mr.
Dumhart:
This is
in response to your letter requesting advice regard
ing your responsibilities as
a Management Assistant
I with the
City of
San Juan Capistrano pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.
2
Please note that nothing
in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition,
this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
Government
Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by
an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114;
Regulation 18329(c) (3) )
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-524
Page
2
QUESTION
May you participate in decisions of the City of San Juan
Capistrano regarding city services that your spouse’s employer,
the Solag Disposal
Company,
has contracted to provide?
CONCLUSION
You may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
the Solag Disposal
Company,
either directly or indirectly.
FACTS
The city is considering the Los Rios Precise Plan.
The plan,
adopted in 1978,
designates the Los Rios area as
a proposed
historic park site.
The precise alan identifies
a large portion
of privately-owned property,
currently owned by the Solag Disposal
Company,
to be used for the proposed historical park.
In 1990,
the redevelopment agency entered into an agreement with Tom
Trulis,
owner of the Solag Disposal Company,
to swap land
purchased by the city in
1988 for the property owned by the Solag
Disposal Company that was designated as part of the historic park.
The Solag Disposal
Company is also the current contractor with the
city to provide waste management services.
You are
a manager in the City of San Juan Capistrano’s City
Manager’s Office.
You are in charge of monitoring and achieving
the city’s compliance with the California Integrated Waste
Management Act of
1989.
As part of your job duties,
you will administer the Solid
Waste Management agreement between the Solag Disposal
Company and
the city,
oversee the relocation of the Solag Disposal
Company
pursuant to the Los Rios Precise Plan,
and implement the city’s
Source Reduction and Recycling Element,
and Household Hazardous
Waste Element.
Your spouse is employed by the Solag Disposal
Company as
a
part—time recycling coordinator.
The recycling coordinator was
required as part of the franchise agreement between the city and
the Solag Disposal
Company.
The recycling coordinator is
responsible for educating and
informing the public,
and tracking
compliance with the California Integrated Waste Management Act of
1989.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in makiflg,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence
a governmental decision in which


Our File No.
1-92-524
Page
3
the official has
a financial interest.
As
a designated employee
of the City of San Juan Capistrano,
you are
a public official
under the Act.
(Section 82048; Regulation 18700.)
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has
a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have
a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
Any source of
income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or
promised to the public officfal within
12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.
Section 87103(c).
In addition,
Section 82030 provides that the
income of an
individual also includes any community property interest in the
income of a spouse.
Consequently,
if any person or business has
been a source of
income to you of $250 or more,
or to your spouse
of $500 or more
(making your community property interest $250)
within the past
12 months,
the source of
income is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest as described in Section 87103.
Your spouse is employed by the Solag Disposal
Company.
Presumably your spouse has received more than $500
from the Solag
Disposal
Company.
Thus,
the Solag Disposal Company is
a source of
income to you and you may not participate in any governmental
decision which will have
a foreseeable and material financial
effect on the Solag Disposal Company.
3
Whether the financial consequences of
a decision are reason
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular decision.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial
likelihood that it will occur..
certainty is not required.
However,
if an effect is only
a mere possibility,
it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
However,
income paid to your spouse as separate property
income,
pursuant to a bona fide separate property agreement,
does
not create an economic interest for you in the sources of the
income since you do not have a community property interest in the
income.
(Vassey Advice Letter,
No.
A-86-201.)


Our File No.
1-92-524
Page
4
You described your duties as Management Assistant I as
administering the Solid Waste Management Agreement between the
Solag Disposal
Company and the city,
overseeing the relocation of
the Solag Disposal
Company pursuant to the Los Rios Precise Plan,
and implementing the city’s Source Reduction and Recycling Element
and Household Hazardous Waste Element.
It is foreseeable that these decisions will financially
affect the Solag Disposal
Company.
However,
the foreseeable
financial effect on your source of income must also be material to
require disqualification.
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the effect of
a decision is material,
based on the
specific circumstances of each decision.
Regulation 18702.1(a)
provides that where a source of
income is directly before your
agency as an applicant or the subject of the decision,
the effect
of the decision is deemed to be material and disqualification is
required.
A source of
income is directly before an agency when the
source initiates the proceeding by filing an application,
claim,
.1,
or similar request,
or is a
named party in,
or the subiect
,
the proceeding.
A source of income is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal,
approval,
denial or revocation of
any license,
permit,
or other entitlement
to,
or contract with,
the subject person or business entity.
4
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
Consequently,
since the various decisions you will be
involved in appear to directly involve the Solag Disposal Company,
you would be disqualified from participating in the decisions.
The “Public Generally” Exception
Even if an official has an economic interest in a decision
which will be materially financially affected by the decision,
the
You are also required to disqualify yourself where the Solag
Disposal
Company will be indirectly affected by
a decision.
Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed)
provides differing dollar
thresholds of materiality depending on the financial size of the
business entity.
For example,
for a relatively small business
entity,
Regulation 18702.2(g)
provides that the effect of a
decision is material if the decision will result in an increase or
decrease in its gross revenues for a fiscal year,
the value of
assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more,
or will result in the
company incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of
$2,500 or more.


Our File No.
1-92-524
Page
5
official may still participate in the decision if the effect on
the official’s economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.
For the “public generally” exception to apply,
the decision
must affect the official’s economic interests in substantially the
same manner as it will affect a significant segment of the
jurisdiction.
(Regulation 18703;
In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
Based upon the information provided,
it would appear that
the potential effects on the Solag Disposal
Company as
a
contractor will
be distinguishable from the effects of the
decision on the public generally.
Thus, it does not appear that
the exception in Regulation
18703 will apply.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact
me at
(916)
322-590l.
Sincerely,
SH:JWW
Enclosures
Scott Hallabrin
cting General Counsel
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many
law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.
John
W.
Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you participate in decisions of the City of San Juan
Capistrano regarding city services that your spouse’s employer,
the Solag Disposal
Company,
has contracted to provide?

CONCLUSION: You may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
the Solag Disposal
Company,
either directly or indirectly.

Durnhart Management Assistant I City of San Juan Capistrano 32400 Paseo Adelanto San Juan Capistrano, CA 92675 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1—92—524 Dear Mr. Dumhart: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard ing your responsibilities as a Management Assistant I with the City of San Juan Capistrano pursuant to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3) ) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1-92-524 Page 2 QUESTION May you participate in decisions of the City of San Juan Capistrano regarding city services that your spouse’s employer, the Solag Disposal Company, has contracted to provide? CONCLUSION You may not participate in any governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Solag Disposal Company, either directly or indirectly. FACTS The city is considering the Los Rios Precise Plan. The plan, adopted in 1978, designates the Los Rios area as a proposed historic park site. The precise alan identifies a large portion of privately-owned property, currently owned by the Solag Disposal Company, to be used for the proposed historical park. In 1990, the redevelopment agency entered into an agreement with Tom Trulis, owner of the Solag Disposal Company, to swap land purchased by the city in 1988 for the property owned by the Solag Disposal Company that was designated as part of the historic park. The Solag Disposal Company is also the current contractor with the city to provide waste management services. You are a manager in the City of San Juan Capistrano’s City Manager’s Office. You are in charge of monitoring and achieving the city’s compliance with the California Integrated Waste Management Act of 1989. As part of your job duties, you will administer the Solid Waste Management agreement between the Solag Disposal Company and the city, oversee the relocation of the Solag Disposal Company pursuant to the Los Rios Precise Plan, and implement the city’s Source Reduction and Recycling Element, and Household Hazardous Waste Element.
